title: Just-In-Time Context Loading
concept: Loading detailed context only when a tool call or sub-query signals it is actually needed, not preemptively.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: In recency-weighted relevance, how does a system treat an old but topically matching document versus a brand-new one on the same topic?

options.text: It blends topic match with how recent the info is, so the older document gets naturally deprioritized
It always picks the older document because age shows reliability
It ignores recency completely and only looks at keyword overlap

exercises.explain: Recency-weighted relevance combines how well something matches the topic with how fresh it is, so stale information naturally sinks lower even if it once matched well. This helps keep answers current.

cards.title: Why Carry What You Don't Need Yet?
cards.body: Imagine packing your entire closet for a weekend trip just in case you might wear something. Most AI systems do this with context too — loading tons of detail before knowing if it's even needed.

cards.title: What Is Just-In-Time Context Loading?
cards.body: It means holding back detailed information until a specific tool call or sub-query signals it's actually needed. Instead of stuffing everything into context upfront, the system waits for a clear trigger, then fetches just that piece.

cards.title: Like an ER, Not a Warehouse
cards.body: An emergency room doesn't wheel every medical device into your room 'just in case.' They bring the X-ray machine only when a doctor actually calls for one. Just-in-time context loading works the same way: fetch the detailed stuff only when a real signal says you need it.

cards.title: A Search Assistant in Action
cards.body: A user asks 'What's the weather in Tokyo?' The assistant doesn't preload weather data for every city on Earth. It waits until it sees the sub-query 'weather + Tokyo,' then calls the weather tool for just that city, right when needed.

cards.title: Myth: More Context Loaded Early = Smarter AI
cards.body: Actually, dumping in extra detail before it's needed can bury the important stuff and slow things down. Waiting for a real signal — like a tool call needing specific data — keeps responses sharper and faster.

cards.title: Preload Everything vs. Load Just In Time
cards.body: Compare packing your whole closet versus packing only what your itinerary calls for, adding items as plans firm up.

left.text: All documents, tools, and data loaded upfront 'just in case,' cluttering context with mostly unused info.

right.text: Only the exact context needed is fetched, triggered by a real tool call or sub-query, keeping things lean and focused.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best demonstrates just-in-time context loading?

options.text: Loading every user's full order history before answering any question, even simple ones
Fetching a customer's shipping address only when the assistant calls the 'track package' tool
Loading the company's entire product catalog into every conversation from the start
Randomly loading different customer records each time to stay 'fresh'

exercises.explain: Option B waits for a clear signal — the 'track package' tool call — before pulling in the shipping address, which is exactly what's needed at that moment. The other options load large amounts of context regardless of whether it's actually needed, wasting resources and risking clutter.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Just-in-time context loading means the AI should load all possibly relevant documents at the very start of a conversation, so it's ready for anything.
exercises.explain: False — just-in-time loading is the opposite of preloading everything. It means waiting for an actual signal, like a tool call or specific sub-query, before fetching the detailed context that's actually needed.

exercises.sentence: In just-in-time context loading, detailed information is fetched only when a tool call or sub-query gives a clear signal that it is needed.
exercises.explain: The whole point of just-in-time loading is waiting for a signal — a clear indication like a specific tool call or sub-query — before fetching detailed context, rather than loading everything preemptively.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three system behaviors was most likely generated by an AI that misunderstood 'just-in-time loading' as 'load everything immediately, all at once'?

options.text: The assistant waits until the user asks about refunds, then loads only the refund policy document.
The assistant preloads the refund policy, shipping policy, warranty terms, and full FAQ the moment the chat starts, before the user has typed anything.
The assistant loads the shipping policy only after detecting a sub-query about a delayed package.

exercises.explain: Option B loads everything upfront regardless of any signal from the user, which is the opposite of just-in-time loading. True just-in-time behavior (like options A and C) waits for a specific trigger — a topic mention or sub-query — before fetching the matching detailed context.

exercises.prompt: An AI coding assistant is asked: 'Fix the typo in my README file.' Which context-loading strategy best follows just-in-time loading?

options.text: Load the entire codebase, all past commits, and every dependency's documentation before touching the README
Load only the README file first, and pull in more (like style guides) only if the task turns out to need them
Load nothing at all and guess the fix without reading any file
Load a random sample of files from the project folder to be safe

exercises.explain: Just-in-time loading means starting with the minimum context that directly matches the signal (here, 'fix a typo in the README' points straight at the README file). You only reach for more context if a new signal appears, like the model realizing it needs a style guide to know the correct wording. Loading the whole codebase (option A) wastes tokens and attention on irrelevant material, loading nothing (C) risks a blind guess, and loading random files (D) has no connection to the actual task signal. This wraps up Unit 421 on Relevance-Based Prioritization: instead of cramming everything into context upfront, smart systems watch for real signals of need — topic match, recency, and now timing — and pull in detail exactly when it's called for, keeping the model focused and efficient.
